title: CHAPTER TWO: THE FROG IN THE WELL
content:
> *"The term 'Great Master' -- zongjang -- carries a weight in the martial world that no translation adequately conveys. It does not mean 'expert' or 'grandmaster' in the common sense. It refers specifically to the founder of a martial art worthy of establishing a sect: a Zhang Sanfeng, a Bodhidharma. To call a person a Great Master is to say that they have created something that will outlive them by centuries. To call a fifteen-year-old boy a Great Master is to invite laughter. Or a beating."*
>
> -- Heon Won-chang, *Collected Letters from Xiangyang* (unsent)

---

Fifteen years later, a boy with thick eyebrows was doing squats with a tree on his back.

Not a branch. Not a log. A section of trunk as long as he was tall and thick enough that a grown man would need both arms to encircle it. It was lashed across his shoulders with hemp rope that had worn grooves into the calluses on his traps, and he rose and fell with the steady, mechanical rhythm of a bellows pump -- down into a horse stance so deep his thighs were parallel to the ground, then up again, exhaling through his nose in controlled bursts that fogged in the pre-dawn air.

One hundred forty-seven. One hundred forty-eight.

Jeong Yeon-shin was not thinking about the number. His body tracked it the way a river tracks its own current -- automatically, without effort, the count running in some back room of his mind while the rest of him attended to more interesting matters.

*Dead zone between the fourth and fifth lumbar vertebrae. The flow hitches there.*

He adjusted. Exhaled at the bottom instead of the top. The compression shifted, the pressure gradient changed, and the hitch vanished.

*Obviously. The manual was written by someone who'd never circulated energy through a dynamic technique.*

"Young master! It's time for the banquet!"

The shout came from across the training ground, where a servant named Liu stood at the entrance with both hands cupped around his mouth and the expression of a man who had drawn the short straw. Which he had. The household servants had developed a lottery system for the duty of fetching the third young master from whatever inhuman training regimen he'd invented this week.

Yeon-shin did not respond. Down. Up. Down. Up. The hemp rope creaked against his traps. His breath fogged in the pre-dawn air.

One hundred fifty-eight. One hundred fifty-nine.

He reached two hundred, held the bottom position for a ten-count, and set the trunk down. It hit the packed earth with a sound like a muffled drum. He straightened, rolled his shoulders, and picked up the sweat-stained top he'd draped over the fence rail.

His body was wrong.

Not wrong in the way that implied error -- wrong in the way that a wolf among dogs is wrong. He was fifteen and not large. Five foot six, narrow-waisted, with the bone structure of a boy who still had growing to do. But the muscles layered over that frame were dense, striated, visible even at rest -- the compact, diamond-hard definition of a body sculpted by internal energy for over a decade. He looked carved from hardwood.

*The Jeong Family Dynamic Technique. Fully established.*

He allowed himself a moment of satisfaction, brief as a candle flame in wind. The martial art he'd created -- or rather, reinvented -- was complete. At least this version of it.

Where the Jeong family's traditional method required sitting cross-legged and breathing like a constipated monk until the qi deigned to accumulate, the Dynamic Technique circulated energy while the body moved. Every squat, every stance, every sword form became a cultivation session. The muscles compressed and channeled internal energy the way a riverbed channels water -- naturally, constantly.

He'd figured it out when he was nine.

He'd tried to share it when he was ten.

"You *created* martial arts?" his eldest brother Jeong Nam-san had said, with the particular blend of contempt and disbelief that Yeon-shin had come to think of as the family's signature seasoning. "You. The third wife's brat. Created martial arts."

"What if someone goes qi-deviant from practicing it?" his second brother Jeong Jung-san had added, helpful as always. "Will you take responsibility when they die?"

"Do you think you have the qualities of a *Great Master*?"

That last one had been the eldest brother again, and it was the one that stuck. Not because it was clever -- Nam-san was many things, but clever was not among them -- but because the mockery was sincere. His brothers genuinely believed that Yeon-shin creating a martial art was as absurd as a pig composing poetry. And underneath the mockery was something worse.

Fear.

Because if the third wife's unwanted son -- the motherless one, the cursed one, the boy who ate his meals alone at the far end of the table -- was capable of something their father's legitimate heirs were not, then the family's entire hierarchy was built on a lie. And lies, in Yeon-shin's experience, made people mean.

He'd stopped sharing after that.

*Their loss*, he thought, pulling the top over his head and tying the sash with the economical movements of long practice. *Though "loss" implies they had something to lose.*

A headache pulsed behind his left eye. Brief, sharp, and then gone -- like a coal touched to the inside of his skull and immediately withdrawn. He pressed two fingers to his temple, waited. Nothing.

It was happening more often.

He chose not to think about that.

---

"Young master!"

"I'm coming."

He said it casually, the way you'd acknowledge a comment about the weather, and let Liu's visible relief wash over him without interest. The servant was afraid -- not of Yeon-shin, but of what the family head would do if the third son was late to the banquet. Again.

The Jeong estate was arranged in the traditional courtyard style: central axis of grand buildings, walled gardens, servant quarters, training grounds. Yeon-shin walked the edges as he always did -- side corridors, secondary paths, the route that minimized the chance of encountering family. A servant carrying firewood saw him and flinched sideways. A maid sweeping the gallery threshold kept her eyes on her broom.

He passed them like a ghost. Ghosts didn't have to smile.

As he passed the guest quarters, the sound of recitation drifted through an open window:

"Recite Chapter One, Section Four of the Great Ming Code."

"The compatriots with long ears and small stature returned from the gates of heaven, destroyed the evil Yuan, and established the Great Ming alongside Taizu."

"Chapter One, Section Eight."

"A giant demon pursued the compatriots, but the gates were sealed in time and it was annihilated."

Yeon-shin smiled faintly. The sanitized version. The one where the elves were noble allies from heaven and "compatriots" wasn't a diplomatic fiction papering over something considerably more complicated.

But that was a thought for scholars, not for a third son walking to a banquet he'd been notified of rather than invited to.

---

The banquet hall smelled of roasted duck, soy-braised pork belly, and ambition.

"You're late, fool."

His eldest brother greeted him from the head of the family seating, narrow eyes compressing to slits above cheeks flushed with wine. Jeong Nam-san was twenty-two, built like a barrel, and possessed of the particular confidence that comes from being the firstborn son of a wealthy family in a world that rewards firstborn sons of wealthy families simply for existing.

The muscles in Yeon-shin's chest twitched -- the Dynamic Technique making him sensitive to the hostility in Nam-san's energy the way a dog feels a storm.

His brother was angry. But underneath the anger was something fragile.

"It's been a while, brother," Yeon-shin said, and bowed exactly the correct amount.

"If you're late, hurry up and sit."

He sat. At the far end of the table, naturally. The position reserved for the lowest-ranking family member, despite the fact that three of his younger half-siblings were present. It was against protocol -- the third son of the main wife should have been seated above the children of concubines -- but protocol had been quietly murdered in the Jeong household years ago, stabbed in the back by the first wife's whisper campaigns and buried in the courtyard of the family head's indifference.

Yeon-shin didn't mind.

*Honestly. No responsibilities, no obligations, no one asking me to attend meetings about grain shipments or tenant disputes. And the food is excellent.*

The food *was* excellent. Roast duck lacquered with honey and five-spice, the skin crackling like parchment. Pork belly that melted on the tongue. Pickled radishes sharp enough to make his eyes water.

He ate. He listened.

"Since my late father, who was a disciple of the Southern Edge Sect, founded our family," the family head was saying, his voice swelling with the grandiose tone he adopted when discussing lineage, "I have devoted my life to reviving our clan. If I had wanted to make a name for myself in the world, I too would have inherited the Taoist techniques! But how could I abandon the family business?"

Yeon-shin reached for a rice cake.

"I am overjoyed that my second son will now follow in his grandfather's footsteps and climb Mount Zhongnan."

This was the reason for the banquet: Jeong Jung-san, the second son, had been accepted as a named disciple of the Southern Edge Sect. One of the Nine Great Sects of the martial world. A significant honor, if you cared about that sort of thing, and the family head cared about that sort of thing the way fish cared about water.

"It's a good day," said the first wife.

"Congratulations, brother," said one of the younger siblings.

"Even the Cloud Sword Dragon will be impressed," Jung-san said, already referring to the Southern Edge Sect's most famous young master as "senior brother," a presumption so premature it would have been funny if it weren't so entirely expected.

Yeon-shin chewed his rice cake and kept his expression neutral.

*You'll spend the first three years cleaning ancestral tablets and carrying water up the mountain. The Southern Edge Sect's internal hierarchy is as rigid as their sword forms, and a named disciple from a secular branch family starts at the absolute bottom. But sure. Senior brother.*

"I hear the third spends all day at the training ground."

The family head's gaze had found him. It was the gaze of a man looking at a stain on his favorite robe -- not angry, exactly. Weary. Irritated by the reminder.

"How is your progress with the Jeong Family Divine Sword?"

The table went quiet. Yeon-shin set down his chopsticks and sat up straight.

"The principles of our family's martial arts are difficult for me to grasp, Father. It's hard to make much progress. I'm ashamed."

The lie came out smooth and warm, like tea poured from a pot. In truth, the Jeong Family Divine Sword was a cobbled-together imitation of the Southern Edge Sect's Thirty-Six Heavenly Swords, assembled by the family head's father with limited understanding and less talent. Yeon-shin had identified seven fundamental flaws in its sword paths before he was twelve. He could have fixed them. He could have rebuilt the entire style from the ground up and produced something twice as effective.

But he'd learned what happened when you showed people what you could do in this family.

"The first and second sons are approaching mastery," the family head said. "Your efforts are... noted. But you should reflect on whether you are truly putting in effort."

"I will heed your advice."

He bowed. The family head's gaze moved on.

Yeon-shin picked up his chopsticks and resumed eating. The pork belly was still warm. The pickled radishes still bit with satisfying sharpness. His chest, if anyone had asked -- which no one did, and no one would -- felt like someone had reached through his ribs and squeezed.

*Showing interest will only hurt me.*

He ate the pork belly.

It helped.

---

"Southern Edge masters have arrived!"

Zhang's voice, shrill with excitement, split the banquet hall like a cleaver through bamboo. In an instant, the careful decorum of the evening shattered into chaos. Chairs scraped. Wine cups toppled. The family head, who had been sitting at the head of the table with the immovable dignity of a mountain, leaped to his feet like a man who'd discovered a scorpion in his boot.

"Quickly -- quickly welcome them! No, I'll go myself!"

Yeon-shin set down his chopsticks.

*The Southern Edge Sect. Here. In Xinye.*

He was not an excitable person. But the Southern Edge Sect was one of the Nine Great Sects. Their sword art was *the* standard against which all Taoist swordsmanship was measured.

And he was, despite everything, still a martial artist.

He followed the crowd to the main gate.

What he saw there stopped him.

Three figures stood in the courtyard, their Taoist robes crisp in the lantern light. Two were men -- middle-aged, hard-eyed, with the confident posture of warriors who had spent decades perfecting their craft. Their energy was clean and sharp, like mountain water.

The third was something else entirely.

Golden hair fell past her shoulders in a straight cascade. Her ears rose to tapered points above a face that was beautiful the way a winter landscape is beautiful -- precise, symmetrical, faintly inhuman. Her eyes were pale grey-green, the color of jade left in rain.

Those eyes had been watching the world for a very long time.

"An elf," Nam-san breathed beside him, the contempt in his voice replaced for once by naked awe. His eldest brother was staring with his mouth slightly open, which under other circumstances Yeon-shin would have found entertaining.

"I am Yeo Il-shin," the elf woman said, her voice carrying the effortless clarity of someone who had never needed to raise it. "An elder of the Southern Edge Sect. We are grateful for your hospitality."

The family head was already prostrating himself. "I -- I failed to recognize an immortal!"

The family members followed suit. Around the courtyard, guests and servants dropped like wheat before a scythe, foreheads pressing to stone. Some of them were weeping.

*Isn't that going too far?*

Yeon-shin bowed. Properly, respectfully, with clasped hands -- but he did not prostrate himself.

The elf's energy hit him like heat from an open forge. Dense. Layered. Refined so many times it had become a different substance entirely, radiating from her in waves that made his skin prickle from fifteen feet away.

*So this is what a true master feels like.*

He raised his head.

And found her looking directly at him.

Their eyes met. Hers were ancient and curious. His were -- he didn't know what his were. Dark, probably. He'd been told he had his mother's eyes, by one of the older servants, once, before the woman had realized what she'd said and retreated as if she'd touched a hot stove.

The elf elder tilted her head. A fraction of a degree. The ghost of interest, there and gone.

Then the family head was ushering the masters inside, and the moment dissolved like mist in morning sun.

Yeon-shin stood in the emptying courtyard and pressed two fingers to his temple, where the headache was pulsing again.

*The Dynamic Technique only trained the body. The body is a vessel. But I never thought about what the vessel was supposed to hold.*

He was grinning. The kind of grin that would have worried anyone who knew him well, if anyone knew him well.

No one did.
